questions.question: Two roommates are deciding whether to buy a shared router that costs $70. Maya values it at $50 and Leo values it at $40. What is the efficient decision, and why might voluntary contribution fail?

choices.A: Buy it; total benefit is $90, but each roommate may prefer the other to pay.
choices.B: Do not buy it; neither individual value exceeds cost.
choices.C: Buy it only if Maya pays the full $70 because her value is higher.
choices.D: Do not buy it because shared goods have zero total benefit.

questions.explanation: The router is efficient because total benefit 50 + 40 = 90 exceeds cost 70. Voluntary contribution may fail because each can benefit if the other pays. B ignores summed benefits. C confuses efficiency with distribution. D is false.

questions.question: A neighborhood security patrol costs $120 per week. Three households value it at $50, $45, and $40 per week. What is efficient, and what does free riding predict about voluntary provision?

choices.A: Provide it; total value is $135, but each household has an incentive to understate willingness to pay.
choices.B: Do not provide it; the highest individual value is below $120.
choices.C: Provide it only if the household valuing it at $50 pays the full cost.
choices.D: Do not provide it because non-rival goods cannot be valuable.

questions.explanation: Total benefit is 135, greater than cost 120, so provision is efficient. Free riding may prevent voluntary financing. B uses a private-good individual condition. C answers a distribution question, not efficiency. D is conceptually wrong.

questions.question: In a two-person public good contribution game, each person receives benefit $60 if the good is provided. The cost of contributing is initially $40 for one contributor, then rises to $80. What happens to the incentive to contribute individually?

choices.A: It weakens; when the private cost exceeds the private benefit, each person prefers to free ride if possible.
choices.B: It strengthens; higher cost makes contribution more socially valuable.
choices.C: It is unchanged because total social benefit is unchanged.
choices.D: It disappears only if both people have identical values.

questions.explanation: A higher private cost makes unilateral contribution less attractive, even if total social benefits could still justify provision. B reverses the incentive effect. C ignores individual payoff comparisons. D introduces irrelevant symmetry.

questions.question: A public radio station has 1,000 listeners, each valuing it at $5. Fixed cost is $3,000. If the listener base rises to 2,000 while individual value remains $5, what changes about efficient provision and free riding?

choices.A: Efficient provision becomes more strongly justified, but voluntary payment may still fail because each listener can hope others pay.
choices.B: Efficient provision becomes less justified because more people make the good more costly to consume.
choices.C: Free riding disappears because a larger group makes exclusion easier.
choices.D: Provision becomes inefficient because individual value remains below fixed cost.

questions.explanation: Total benefit rises from $5,000 to $10,000 while cost stays $3,000, so provision is efficient in both cases and more valuable with more listeners. But larger groups can worsen free riding. B treats the good as rival. C is not implied. D uses individual rather than aggregate benefit.

questions.question: In a graph of voluntary public good provision, the social marginal benefit curve lies above every individual's marginal benefit curve. What does this vertical gap represent?

choices.A: The benefits to other people that an individual does not consider when deciding whether to contribute.
choices.B: The marginal cost of producing the public good.
choices.C: The amount by which the good is rival.
choices.D: The tax revenue collected from users.

questions.explanation: For a public good, an individual's contribution benefits others too. The gap between social and private marginal benefit captures ignored benefits to others. B is a cost curve, not the benefit gap. C confuses rivalry with external benefit. D is not shown by the benefit curves.

questions.question: A public good graph shows the efficient quantity where summed marginal benefits cross MC, but voluntary contributions stop where one person's marginal benefit crosses MC. What does the graph illustrate?

choices.B: Underprovision because contributors ignore benefits received by non-contributors.
choices.C: Efficient provision because one person's demand is enough.
choices.D: No market failure because marginal cost is positive.

questions.explanation: Voluntary contributors base decisions on private benefits, not total benefits, so public goods tend to be underprovided. A reverses the typical result. C ignores summed benefits. D incorrectly says positive marginal cost prevents market failure.

questions.question: Why can free riding be a Nash equilibrium in a voluntary public good contribution game?

choices.A: Because each person may prefer not contributing regardless of whether others contribute, while still benefiting if others pay.
choices.B: Because everyone receives zero benefit from the public good.
choices.C: Because each person is legally required to contribute.
choices.D: Because public goods are rival in consumption.

questions.explanation: Free riding is individually rational when a person can enjoy the good without paying and their own contribution is costly. B denies the benefit. C removes voluntariness. D misclassifies public goods.

questions.question: A class can buy a shared online study guide. Everyone can access it once purchased, whether or not they paid. Several students say they will wait to see if others buy it. Which concept best explains this?

choices.A: Free riding in public good provision.
choices.B: Diminishing marginal utility for private goods.
choices.D: Tragedy of the commons from rivalry.

questions.explanation: The study guide is non-excludable within the class once purchased, so students have an incentive to benefit without contributing. B is not the strategic issue. C is not about seller pricing. D applies to rival non-excludable resources.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes free riding from the tragedy of the commons?

choices.A: Free riding causes underprovision of non-rival public goods; tragedy of the commons causes overuse of rival common resources.
choices.B: Free riding causes overuse of rival resources; tragedy of the commons causes underprovision of public goods.
choices.C: Free riding occurs only with private goods; tragedy of the commons occurs only with club goods.
choices.D: Free riding and tragedy of the commons are identical because both require excludability.

questions.explanation: Free riding is about not paying for a non-excludable public good; commons problems are about overusing a rival non-excludable resource. B reverses the concepts. C misclassifies both. D ignores that both often arise from non-excludability, not excludability.

questions.question: A public good costs $100. Four people value it at $40, $35, $20, and $15. Voluntary contributions fail and the good is not provided. What is the deadweight loss from non-provision?

choices.B: $10 because total value exceeds cost by $10.
choices.C: $100 because the cost is not paid.

questions.explanation: Total benefit is 40 + 35 + 20 + 15 = 110, and cost is 100. Efficient provision would create net surplus of 10, so failure to provide creates DWL of 10. A ignores forgone surplus. C counts cost rather than net loss. D ignores the cost saved by not providing.
